title: Engineering
scan: What this letter is: The E of CODE — the engineering domain, where business intent is transformed into working software
Why it matters: Unlike the other three CODE letters, Engineering doesn't just carry questions — it carries its own five-layer framework, CODER
Engineering telescopes: zoom into the E of CODE and a second framework unfolds — C · O · D · E · R — five letters for keeping the build itself honest
Deliverables at this level: technical architecture, the CODER plan, and working software
This chapter is the bridge — it opens into CODER, where Code, Optimization & Quality, Deployment, External Integrations, and Release Management each get their own deep-dive

blocks.html: ngineering transforms business intent into working software. Unlike the other three, this letter doesn't just have questions — it has its own five-layer framework. Zoom in below.

sections.title: Engineering expands into C · O · D · E · R

blocks.html: Engineering expands into C · O · D · E · R
Zoom into the E of CODE and a second framework unfolds — this time with five letters. It's how an engineering lead keeps the build itself honest.

nodes.label: C · Code
nodes.sub: FE · BE · integration · project management. This is where all of Part I lives.

nodes.label: O · Optimization & Quality
nodes.sub: Performance, testing, QA — making it fast and making it correct.

nodes.label: D · Deployment & Docs
nodes.sub: Shipping it, plus authoring, documentation & the build/support layer.

nodes.label: E · External Integrations
nodes.sub: Third parties, APIs, partner systems — engagement beyond your own walls.

nodes.label: R · Release Management
nodes.sub: Versioning, rollout, cadence — the discipline of when things go live.

items: Code — Are models, functions and events identified and documented? (Part I is the deep-dive.)
Optimization & Quality — Are performance targets set and quality gates established? Will it hold at 100 users — and at 100,000?
Deployment — Is it automated? Can we roll back? Are environments aligned?
External Integrations — What systems are involved, what happens when they fail, and who owns them?
Release Management — Is release readiness established, is hypercare planned, is success measurable?

blocks.html: Most digital failures are release failures, not engineering failures. Change reaching production safely is its own discipline — that's why R exists.
